---
title: "Week 8 — Defense and JSSR Submission"
subtitle: "ASSIP 8-Week Empirical Finance Camp"
author: "Prof. Lei Gao, George Mason University"
date: "2026-08-10"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
format:
  pptx:
    slide-level: 2
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    fig-width: 7.5
    fig-height: 4.2
    fig-dpi: 200
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto
bibliography: ../bibliography.md
---

# Week 8 — Defense and JSSR Submission {.title}

::: notes
Welcome to Week 8 of the ASSIP Empirical Finance Camp. This is the last
active week of the program, and on Wednesday, August twelfth, you will
stand beside a forty-eight by thirty-six inch research poster at the
ASSIP Symposium and defend, in eight minutes, the result you have spent
seven weeks building. The pre-analysis plan you tagged in Week 7 is no
longer a document. It is a contract that comes due this week. You will
run the pre-registered specification once, build a one-hundred-forty-four
specification multiverse around it, try to kill your own result with
placebos and bootstraps, write a two-hundred-fifty-word JSSR abstract
that compresses the whole project into one paragraph, and then face a
roomful of faculty whose job is to find the reason not to believe you.
This deck previews all five chapters. The reveal that runs through them
is the same: a result you have already attacked is the only kind worth
defending in public.
:::

## Learning Objectives

- Execute the pre-registered specification once, and report whatever it shows
- Build a specification curve that makes the garden of forking paths visible
- Try to kill your own result with placebos, bootstraps, and Oster delta
- Write a two-hundred-fifty-word JSSR abstract and a six-panel poster
- Defend the result for eight minutes against a hostile referee

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
A result that survives every honest attack you can mount on it before
the symposium is the only kind that survives the attacks the room
mounts on you during the symposium.
:::

::: notes
Notice the verbs: execute, build, kill, write, defend. None of them is
explore. By this week the design is fixed and the question is fixed.
What changes is whether you can hold the result up to a stranger and
say honestly what you found, what you did not find, and what you cannot
yet rule out. Maya, Devon, Priya, Sam, and Leah will all walk this same
arc with you. By Wednesday morning the JSSR abstract is generated from
code, the poster compiles with one command, and the talk has been
rehearsed against a timer three times. Everything else this week is in
service of those three artifacts being ready.
:::

## The Confirmatory Run

- Open the notebook gated by the `PAP_FILED=True` flag
- Run the one specification your `pap-filed` commit named — exactly once
- Report the number whichever way it falls
- Maya's run: ATT of negative one point eight nine percentage points

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The PAP froze the forks. The confirmatory run honors that freeze. The
sin is not deviating; the sin is hiding the deviation or letting the
outcome steer the change.
:::

::: notes
Here is the result in one sentence. Your first job this week is to run
the pre-registered primary specification once, on the confirmation
data, and report what it produces whatever the sign. This is the
hardest professional habit in the entire course, and it is
psychological, not technical. Recall from Chapter one point five that a
p-value of zero point zero five is only a five percent false-positive
rate if the test was fixed before you saw the outcome. The instant you
look at the coefficient, dislike it, and tweak the specification to
improve it, that contract is broken and your p-value means something
larger and unknowable. Maya's notebook is technologically gated behind
a flag that must be asserted before the analysis cell will run. When
she flipped the gate, the estimator returned an ATT of negative one
point eight nine percentage points with a clustered standard error of
zero point zero six five. That is the number she reports, full stop.
:::

## The Garden of Forking Paths, Made Visible

- Enumerate every defensible analytic choice — controls, fixed effects, sample, clustering, outcome
- Maya: four controls times two fixed-effects choices times three samples times three clusterings times two outcomes — one hundred forty-four specifications
- Run all of them, plot estimates sorted, choices in a grid below
- The pre-registered specification is one labeled point inside the multiverse

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
P-hacking exploits multiplicity by concealment. Specification-curve
analysis defangs multiplicity by exhaustive disclosure.
:::

::: notes
The pre-analysis plan protects your headline, but it leaves one
question hanging that the plan cannot answer. How many of the other
defensible paths would have given you the same answer? Simonsohn,
Simmons, and Nelson's specification curve answers that by refusing to
hide. You list every defensible analytic choice in each of the seven
spec-discipline slots, you multiply them out, and for Maya that gives
exactly one hundred forty-four specifications. Each is a single point
in the multiverse. You run all of them, mechanical and fast, and you
plot the estimates sorted from most negative to most positive on the
top panel, with a grid below showing which choices produced each point.
The genius of the picture is the bottom panel. You can read downward
from any interesting region of the curve and see exactly which fork
created it. The garden of forking paths is no longer hidden. It is on
the slide.
:::

## Reading the Specification Curve

- Where does the sign live? Does it flip across specifications?
- Where does the pre-registered estimate sit — center or tail?
- What fraction is significant, and where do the hollow markers cluster?
- Which row of the bottom panel best separates the curve?

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
A flat curve sitting below zero with the pre-registered point at its
median says robust. A curve sweeping from significantly negative
through zero to significantly positive says the result is an artifact
of analytic choice.
:::

::: notes
Reading the curve is the skill. Maya's reads as a flat sweep from
about negative two point four to negative zero point four percentage
points, median around negative one point eight, with one hundred
thirty-two of one hundred forty-four specifications significant. The
twelve that are not all combine the wild cluster bootstrap with the
most aggressive sample trim — the lowest-power corner. Sign never
flips. Now contrast Sam's momentum curve. It sweeps from significantly
negative on the left through a fat band of insignificance to
significantly positive on the right, and every significant positive
specification shares one choice: equal-weighted, microcaps included.
Reading downward in one glance tells the whole story. The anomaly is
not a feature of the cross-section of returns. It is a feature of how
you weight tiny illiquid stocks. The curve drags that into the light
and forces Sam's honest sentence onto the page.
:::

## Trying to Kill the Result

- Placebos — fake dates, fake units, fake outcomes
- Alternative standard errors — cluster by county, by state, two-way
- Wild cluster bootstrap when treated clusters are few
- Multiple-testing correction across the outcome family — Benjamini-Hochberg

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
A robustness section organized around the reader's doubts disarms
attacks before they land. A robustness section that only contains
passes signals you did not try hard enough to fail.
:::

::: notes
Chapter eight point two turns the discipline adversarial. You stop
varying your choices and start attacking your inference itself.
Devon's wild cluster bootstrap exists because his treated-cluster count
is small, and the conventional clustered standard error over-rejects
when clusters are few. The bootstrap flips the sign of each cluster's
residuals at random under the null and rebuilds the distribution of
the t-statistic. If the bootstrap p-value agrees with the conventional
p-value, your significance is real. If the bootstrap says zero point
one eight where the table said zero point zero one, you do not keep
the stars. Priya's placebo on a fake treatment year three years before
any state actually adopted is her parallel-trends test in disguise.
Leah's family of eight outcomes gets the Benjamini-Hochberg correction
because the chance of at least one false positive across eight true
nulls is one in three. The honest report names the family size and the
correction explicitly.
:::

## Oster Delta: The Confounder You Did Not Measure

- $\hat\delta$ = how strong unobservable selection must be, relative to observable selection, to explain away the effect
- $\hat\delta \ge 1$ means a confounder would have to be at least as strong as everything you observed
- Maya's run: coefficient moves from $-2.2$ to $-1.4$ while $R^2$ climbs from $0.08$ to $0.42$
- $\hat\delta \approx 4.7$ — robust

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Coefficient stability alone proves nothing. Stability relative to how
much explanatory power the controls added is what matters. Oster's
delta makes the comparison precise.
:::

::: notes
Oster confronts the deepest threat — an omitted variable you never
measured. The naive idea is that if your coefficient barely moves when
you add controls, it must be robust. Oster shows that is only half the
story. A coefficient that stays put while R-squared jumps from zero
point one zero to zero point five five is far more reassuring than one
that stays put while R-squared barely rises, because in the first case
the controls were powerful and the coefficient shrugged them off, and
in the second the controls were toothless and proved nothing. You
read three numbers off two regressions, you fix an R-max at one point
three times the controlled R-squared, and you solve for the delta that
would drive your effect to zero. Maya's delta comes out at four point
seven, meaning an unobserved confounder would have to be roughly five
times as predictive as her full applicant-composition controls to
explain away the effect. That is implausible enough to defend in
print.
:::

## The JSSR Abstract: Two Hundred Fifty Words

- Hook — one sentence with a concrete stake
- Question — one sentence
- Data and design — name the identifying assumption verb
- Headline number in human units, with confidence interval
- Strongest robustness fact
- One sentence on what you are not claiming

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The abstract is an introduction at one-twentieth scale. The four moves
of Chapter eight point three — hook, contribution, "we find" preview,
positioning — compress to ten sentences without losing the discipline.
:::

::: notes
The JSSR abstract lives permanently in the Journal of Student
Scientists' Research index whether or not you submit a full paper. It
is the single most-read paragraph of your entire ASSIP. The shape is
fixed and the discipline is the same one Chapter eight point three
trains for an introduction, just compressed. You open with a concrete
stake — Maya does not open with "fair lending has been studied"; she
opens with "mortgage applicants from minority neighborhoods were
denied at nearly twice the rate of applicants from white
neighborhoods." You state the question, then the data and design, with
the identifying-assumption verb calibrated to your design. You give
the headline number in units a human can feel — "one point eight
percentage points, about a fifth of the pre-period gap" — with its
confidence interval. You name the strongest robustness fact in one
sentence. And you close with one sentence on what you are not
claiming. The abstract is generated from code, never retyped, so it
cannot drift from the analysis.
:::

## The Six-Panel Poster, the Eight-Minute Arc

- Question — one sentence a sixteen-year-old can repeat back
- Why it matters — one concrete stake
- Design and identification — the assumption, in plain English
- Headline result — one number with its confidence interval, shown as a picture
- Robustness — the specification curve
- Contribution — what is now known, and what you are not claiming

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
One beat per panel, one panel per minute, with two minutes of slack
for the talk to run long. Rehearse it three times against a timer and
cut until it fits in seven minutes, not eight.
:::

::: notes
Eight minutes is not a short version of a long talk. It is a different
object with room for exactly one idea defended cleanly. Memorize the
six-beat arc: question, why it matters, design, headline, robustness,
contribution. One beat per slide, one slide per panel on the poster,
roughly one minute per beat. The discipline that separates a paper
from a talk is the table-to-figure translation. A live audience cannot
read a five-column regression table while you speak. A single
coefficient with its interval becomes a point with an error bar.
Coefficients across specifications become a coefficient plot.
Difference-in-differences becomes an event-study plot. Same number,
two encodings, chosen for the medium. A table is for a reader who can
stop and study; a figure is for a listener who cannot. Sam's momentum
talk does not show the Fama-MacBeth machinery; it shows one sentence
and one small picture of the sort.
:::

## Defending Identification Under Questions

- Survivable critique attacks a number — "did you cluster correctly?"
- Fatal critique attacks the comparison — "your groups were already diverging"
- For each row of your threats table: prepare the question, the test, the residual concern
- Honest "I don't know," located, beats any bluff

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The questions you will get are the rows of your Chapter seven point
five threats table. You wrote them down weeks ago. You are not
improvising — you are reading from a script you authored when you
were calm.
:::

::: notes
The talk is eight minutes; the questions are where the talk is
actually judged. The good news is that you already did the work. Take
your threats-and-responses table and turn each row into a prepared
answer: yes, that is the central worry; if that confounder were
driving this, we would see this signature; we checked, and we see this
reassuring result; what I still cannot fully rule out is this, though
the bound on how bad it could be is this. When the question lands you
answer in that order. The professional move when you get a question
you cannot answer is to say "I don't know" with structure — not a
bare shrug, but a located one. Maya, asked whether her effect would
generalize to auto loans, does not invent a number; she says her data
is mortgages, the mechanism is examiner attention, and she would
expect it to matter wherever there is a comparable exam regime, but
she would not extend without rerunning. That is a better answer than
a confident yes.
:::

## Common Pitfalls

1. Treating a failed robustness test as a setback to be buried, not a finding
2. Letting verbs drift across sections — abstract hedges, conclusion claims "we prove"
3. Showing a regression table on a slide nobody can read in four seconds
4. Bluffing through a question instead of saying a located "I don't know"
5. Defending a fatal identification critique that the room can see is broken

::: callout-warning
## Watch out
Every one of these makes a result that survived the math collapse under
the eyes of the audience. The math was the easy part.
:::

::: notes
Each pitfall has a story. Devon almost buried a failed wild-cluster
bootstrap until his mentor caught it; the honest paper says "the
estimate is suggestive but imprecisely estimated" and earns more
credibility than the three stars he wanted to keep. Leah caught her
own verb drift on a final read-through — the abstract said "is
associated with," the conclusion said "we have shown causes." She
walked every verb back to match her weakest assumption. Priya
translated every results table into a figure for the talk and kept the
tables in backup slides for questioners who wanted the fourth
decimal. Sam practiced one located "I don't know" out loud the night
before until he could say it without flinching. And Maya rehearsed
her concession line for the fatal critique that never came — but she
walked in knowing exactly which retreat position she would take if it
did. That is the professional posture: prepared for the worst, calm
in the room.
:::

## This Week's Deliverable and Look-Ahead

- JSSR abstract — two hundred fifty words, generated from code
- Research poster — six panels, forty-eight by thirty-six landscape, GMU template
- Replication packet — one command rebuilds every number from raw data
- Eight-minute symposium talk — Wednesday, August twelfth
- Optional: full paper for students pushing toward an undergraduate journal

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The repository is the submission. The first thing a JSSR reviewer or
a symposium judge does is clone it and type one command. Everything
else is downstream of whether that command works.
:::

::: notes
There is no separate final upload. The repository you have been
committing into since Lab 7 is the submission, with the `pap-filed`
tag still in its history, the confirmatory commit dated after it,
deviations log filled in, the compiled poster PDF and the JSSR
abstract both regenerable by the same one-command build. The capstone
is two hundred points across four deliverables, and the standard is
the one this book has pressed since Chapter one point five. A modest
result you have stress-tested honestly outscores an ambitious one you
cannot defend. A capstone that writes, unprompted, that its Oster
delta is zero point four and reports the result as a robust
association rather than a clean causal effect earns more than one
that overclaims from the same data. Eight weeks ago you computed an
average return and thought you had an answer. Wednesday you stand
beside a poster and defend a result. That is the arc. See you at the
symposium.
:::

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

::: notes
References are auto-populated by Pandoc from the cited keys. The full
bibliography for Week 8 — Simonsohn, Simmons, and Nelson 2020;
Cameron, Gelbach, and Miller 2008 and 2011; Bertrand, Duflo, and
Mullainathan 2004; Petersen 2009; Oster 2019; Benjamini and Hochberg
1995; Elnahas, Gao, Hossain, and Kim 2024; Gao and Sun 2019 — lives
in the textbook's bibliography file and in each chapter's footnote
block.
:::
